questions.stem: You dose 18 g of ground coffee and want a classic 1:2 espresso. What beverage weight should you stop the shot at?
options: 27 g in the cup, weighed with the portafilter
36 g in the cup, weighed on a cup scale
45 g in the cup, weighed after the crema settles
questions.explanation: Ratio is dose to beverage weight: 18 g × 2 = 36 g out. 27 g is a 1:1.5 ristretto pull, and 45 g is a 1:2.5 lungo — both are ratios, just not the classic 1:2 the lesson targets. Always weigh the cup, not the portafilter.

questions.source: Lesson 0002 — Dialing In a New Bag
questions.stem: A fresh bag pulls 18 g → 36 g in 18 seconds and tastes sour. Keeping the dose fixed, what is the single adjustment to make first?
options: Grind finer so the same shot runs closer to 25–30 seconds
Grind coarser so the same shot runs faster and brighter
Raise the brew temperature two degrees on the next shot
questions.explanation: Fast + sour = under-extraction, and grind size is the first variable you move while dialing in — one change at a time. Coarser makes under-extraction worse, and temperature is a second-order tweak you only reach for after time is in range.

questions.source: Lesson 0002 — Dialing In a New Bag
questions.stem: Your 1:2 shot runs in 20 seconds and tastes sour. Explain what is happening in the cup and, keeping the dose fixed, what you would change for the next shot and why.
questions.modelAnswer: The shot is under-extracted: it ran too fast, so water did not dissolve enough of the coffee's sweetness, leaving sour acids dominant. The fix is to grind finer while keeping the dose and the 1:2 ratio fixed, which slows the flow toward the 25–30 second range and raises extraction on the next shot.
criteria: Links the sour taste to too little dissolved from the grounds
Proposes grinding finer as the next single change
Keeps dose and ratio fixed while adjusting
questions.explanation: Fast and sour is the classic under-extraction signature; grind is the dialing-in lever, moved one shot at a time.
